coredata.dc:title: S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots as an effective fluorescence probe for sensing of furazolidone after magnetic solid-phase microextraction using magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes
coredata.dc:creator: Saadatmandi Manshadi, Sepideh
Dadfarnia, Shayessteh
Haji Shabani, Ali Mohammad
Afsharipour, Roya
Jabbaran, Sadegh
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N-GQDs) was synthesized and a selective measurement method was designed for the quantification of furazolidone based on fluorescence quenching of synthesized quantum dots. Furazolidone was first separated and preconcentrated from samples with complex matrices by magnetic solid-phase microextraction using magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MMWCNTs) as the sorbent. The effective parameters on the extraction of furazolidone were investigated and optimized by one variable at a time and central composite design methods. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence quenched linearly with the furazolidone concentration in the range of 8.0–250.0 µg L−1 with a limit of detection (LOD) 2.1 µg L−1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for intra-day and inter-day analysis (at 40.0 μg L−1 of furazolidone, n = 6) was 3.6 and 5.6%, respectively. The developed method was successfully applied to the quantification of trace amounts of furazolidone in chicken liver, biological fluids, and poultry effluent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Furazolidone
Fluorescence probe
S, N-GQDs
Magnetic multiwall carbon nanotubes
Magnetic solid-phase microextraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical and structural characterization of S, N-GQDs
Optimization of the effective parameters on the determination of FZD
The effect of S, N-GQDs volume
The effect of contact time between FZD and S, N-GQDs
Application of the method for real samples
Comparison of the developed method with other methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of extraction and measurement of FZD.
(a) Absorption spectrum of S, N-GQDs, (b) TEM image of S, N-GQDs, (c) FT-IR spectrum of S, N-GQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrums of MWCNTs-ox, MMWCNTs, FZD, and FZD-MMWCNTs, (b) FESEM image of MMWCNTs, (c) VSM curves of MMWCNTs.

3-D plots for the FZD determination, (a) pH and time of extraction (min), (b) Amount of sorbent (mg) and pH, (c) Time of extraction (min) and eluent volume (µL), (d) Eluent volume (µL) and desorption time.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the developed fluorescence probe to different concentrations of furazolidone, (b) The linear relationships between analytical signal and the furazolidone concentration in the range of 8.0–250.0 µg L−1.
Parameters and their levels with experimental results for determination of FZD.

row.entry: (X1) pH(X2) Mass of sorbent (mg)(X3) Extraction time (min)(X4) Desorption time (s)(X5) Eluent volume (µL)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Test for comparison of the model variance with residual (error) variance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of FZD in real samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean and relative standard deviation of three independent analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the propose method with other methods for determination of the FZD.

row.entry:
Water samples- chicken tissues
Water samples
Water samples

Chicken liver
-Human serum- urine and poultry effluent

head.ce:title: S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots as an effective fluorescence probe for sensing of furazolidone after magnetic solid-phase microextraction using magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N-GQDs) was synthesized and a selective measurement method was designed for the quantification of furazolidone based on fluorescence quenching of synthesized quantum dots. Furazolidone was first separated and preconcentrated from samples with complex matrices by magnetic solid-phase microextraction using magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MMWCNTs) as the sorbent. The effective parameters on the extraction of furazolidone were investigated and optimized by one variable at a time and central composite design methods. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence quenched linearly with the furazolidone concentration in the range of 8.0–250.0 µg L−1 with a limit of detection (LOD) 2.1 µg L−1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for intra-day and inter-day analysis (at 40.0 μg L−1 of furazolidone, n = 6) was 3.6 and 5.6%, respectively. The developed method was successfully applied to the quantification of trace amounts of furazolidone in chicken liver, biological fluids, and poultry effluent.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive consumption of antibiotics in livestock and poultry for treatment and dietary supplementation has increased the possibilities of their residues in animal tissues and entering the human food cycle which endangers public health by creating bacterial resistance. Therefore, in many countries, strict monitoring programs for antibiotics are in place. Nitrofurans are a wide range of synthetic antibiotics affecting gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria and are commonly used for treatments of both humans and animals. Furazolidone (FZD) is one of the nitrofuran antibiotics that is widely used as a growth stimulant in the livestock and poultry industry [1,2]. It is also used for the prevention and treatment of gastrointestinal infections caused by Escherichia coli and Salmonella in birds, especially chickens and roosters. However, despite its strong antibacterial properties, furazolidone residues are recognized to be mutagenic and carcinogenic [2]. So, since 1991, the Food and Drug Administration has refused the prescription of furazolidone and nitrofurans, and in 1995 the European Union banned the use of furazolidone in food-producing animals [3-6]. However, because of its low price, availability, and strong effectiveness, furazolidone is still illegally used in many livestock, poultry, and aquaculture farms. Thus, it is necessary to develop methods for sensitive and selective screening of furazolidone residues in biological tissues and food products. Various analytical techniques such as spectrophotometry [7,8], electrochemistry [9,10], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11-13], chemiluminescence [14,15], and fluorimetry [16,17] has been applied for the determination of FZD. Among the aforementioned methods, recently, the fluorescence technique based on quantum dots has attracted much attention due to the advantages of high selectivity and sensitivity, simplicity, excellent performance, high signal to noise, narrow emission spectrum, low detection limit, and wide linear range. In this regard, in 2021, Zhang et al. prepared carbon dots from coix seed by hydrothermal method and designed a selective nanosensor with a detection limit of 0.096 µM for the determination of FZD [16]. Cai and coworkers also developed a copper nanoclusters fluorescence sensor with a detection limit of 0.012 µM for the determination of FZD in bovine serum. The copper nanoclusters were prepared by a facile and green method using glutathione, and ascorbic acid as the protection and the reducing reagent [17]. The fluorescence intensity of copper nanoclusters decreased proportionally to the amount of FZD that was used as the analytical signal. A review of the literature revealed that so far there is no report on the use of the graphene quantum dots as a sensor for the measurement of FZD.

Graphene quantum dots are a new type of zero-dimensional luminescent nanoparticles based on carbon aromatic sheets that are extensively utilized for the determination of different biomolecules and drugs [18-22]. In these nanoparticles, the carbon atoms are arranged in a honeycomb lattice with sp2/sp3 hybrids and the oxygen-containing functional groups are placed on their surface. The approximate diameter of these nanoparticles is within the range of 3–20 nm. Graphene quantum dots possess excellent properties of biocompatibility, low toxicity, great water solubility, with the possibility of surface modification by various groups, but suffer from low quantum yield [23,24]. To overcome this limitation, their surface has been doped with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphor [25-28]. Chemical doping with heteroatoms changes the semiconductor electron density, increases visible light absorption, improves the optical-electronic properties, and increases the luminescence efficiency.

Furthermore, monitoring and quantification of drugs in biological samples usually required a separation and enrichment step because of the complexity of sample matrices and a very low concentration of the analyte. Magnetic solid-phase microextraction (MSPE), a new mode of solid-phase extraction (SPE), is a powerful separation technique that is widely employed because of its simplicity of phase separation, the capability of combination with an analytical instrument, and low use of organic solvents [29]. However, the selection of sorbent plays a key role in extraction selectivity and efficiency of any mode of solid-phase extraction. Among various sorbents used for MSPE, magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes showed great potential for the sorption of organic molecules due to large surface area, great thermal and mechanical stability, high penetrance, and capability for π-π interaction and ease of its separation from the aqueous samples by a magnetic field that eliminate the time-consuming centrifugation and filtration steps [30-35].

This study aims to design a combined extraction and fluorescence method for the determination of low amounts of furazolidone in complex biological matrices. Thus, magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes were prepared and used for the MSPE of FZD from biological samples as well as poultry tissues and effluent. Then, the extracted FZD was determined with a fluorescent nanoprobe fabricated by sulfur and nitrogen-graphene quantum dots (S, N-GQDs). The FZD quenched the fluorescent intensity of S, N-GQDs probably through the electrostatic interactions or the formation of a hydrogen bond. The quenching of fluorescent intensity was proportional to FZD concentration which was used for the quantification of FZD. The effective parameters on the extraction and quantification of FZD were optimized by one variable at a time method and the central composite design (CCD).

Furazolidone, cystine (%99), ethylene glycol (EG), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (diameter of 5–20 nm, number of walls 3–15, length of 1–10 μm, purity greater than 95 wt%) were prepared from the Research Institute of the Petroleum Industry (Tehran, Iran). Methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), acetonitrile (ACN), dimethylformamide (DMF), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO3) (%65), and ammonia solution (%25), all with analytical grade were prepared from Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany). The stock standard solution of FZD (50 mg L−1) was prepared by dissolving 2.5 mg of pure FZD in 1 mL of ACN and diluting sulfur with double distilled water to 50 mL. This solution was stored at 4 °C. The working standard solutions were daily prepared by appropriate dilution of the stock solution.

A spectrofluorophotometer (Teifsanje Tajhiz Pishraftheh Co. Ltd., model FL-Ar, Iran,) equipped with the deuterium/tungsten excitation source and a photodiode array detection system was used for recording the fluorescence emission spectrum of S-N GQDs and fluorescence measurements. An ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrophotometer (model SPECORD 250, Analytic Jena AG, Germany) was used for recording the absorption spectra and measurement of absorption. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by Bruker Vector 22 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker Equinox 55, Germany) using KBr pellets. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by a Zeiss-EM10C electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany) with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) measurements were carried out by a ZEISS Sigma VP FESEM (ZEISS, Germany). Absorbent magnetic power was determined by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). The pH adjustment was done by an inolab pH meter (model 7110, Germany). A Heidolph mechanical stirrer (Finetech, Korea MR3002) was used for stirring the sample solutions. A permanent magnet (1.2 T, 10 cm × 5 cm × 2 cm Nd2Fe12B) was used for magnetic separation of the sorbent.

For the synthesis of S, N-GQDs, the L- cysteine amino acid was used as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur source and ethylene glycol as the solvent. For the preparation of S, N-GQDs, 10.0 mL of ethylene glycol was added to 5.0 g of cysteine and heated up to 190 °C. The mixture was then stirred at 250 rpm for 20 min. The final product was a brownish suspension which after cooling to room temperature and centrifuging at 6000 rpm, the particles of S, N-GQDs with uniform size distribution were obtained. The schematic of preparation of S, N-GQDs is shown in Fig. S1.

The Fe3O4@MWCNTs nanocomposite was synthesized in two steps as was recorded previously [36]. Firstly, the MWCNTs were oxidized to remove surface impurities and activate the carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups for further interaction. For this purpose, 0.5 g of MWCNTs were added to 70.0 mL of concentrated nitric acid and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. The suspension was then refluxed under magnetic stirring at 70 °C for 6 h. Finally, the oxidized multiwalled carbon nanotubes were separated by centrifugation, washed several times with distilled water until the pH of the solution became neutral, and dried in an oven at 50 °C.

Secondly, the Fe3O4@MWCNTs were prepared by the coprecipitation method [36]. Thus, 0.5 g of oxidized MWCNTs was added to 100.0 mL of distilled water and heated to 80 °C under stirring. Then. 0.2 g FeCl3·6H2O along with 0.7 g FeCl2·4H2O were added to this mixture under nitrogen atmosphere, followed by dropwise addition of 10.0 mL concentrated ammonia solution (25% w/v) (approximately 30 min). After the completion of the reaction, the resulting black precipitate was collected with the help of an external magnet, washed several times with water and ethanol, and dried at 50 °C in an oven. The resulting MMWCNTs sorbent was kept in a closed container for later experiments. The schematic of preparation of Fe3O4@MWCNTs is shown in Fig. S2.
Water sample: The poultry effluent was filtered to remove suspended particles. The furazolidone was then extracted and measured according to the designed procedure (section 2.6).

Biological fluids: Plasma and urine samples were stored frozen until use. To determine the FZD, the frozen sample was thawed to room temperature just before the analysis. The urine sample was centrifuged for15 minutes, filtered, and diluted with a ratio of (1: 1) to reduce the matrix effect. To prepare the plasma sample, 3 mL of the sample was treated with 2 mL of acetonitrile and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. Then, it was filtered and the supernatant was diluted with distilled water to 25 mL in a volumetric flask [37]. Finally, the furazolidone content of urine and plasma samples was extracted and measured according to the proposed method (section 2.6).

Chicken liver: To prepare the liver sample, the frozen sample was thawed at room temperature, and to 200.0 mg of it 3 mL of 1- propanol was added. The mixture was placed in an ice bath, homogenized with a mortar followed by vortexing for 1 min. It was then sonicated and centrifuged for 15 min at 5000 rpm [38]. The supernatant was passed through a 450 nm filter and diluted to a volume of 150 mL with double distilled water. The amount of furazolidone in the sample was extracted and measured according to the designed method.

Tablet sample: Ten 100.0 mg furazolidone tablets were powdered and mixed well. 0.0254 g of it equivalent to one-tenth of the weight of one tablet was dissolved in 10 mL of acetonitrile and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 2 min. The obtained solution was then passed through a filter and diluted properly with distilled to get to the linear range of the method. The analyte was extracted and measured according to the given procedure in section 2.6.

The pH of 150 mL of sample or standard solution containing a maximum amount of 37.5 µg FZD was adjusted at 2.5 with 0.1 mol L−1 of hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide. 30.0 mg of the synthesized MMWCNTs sorbent was added to the solution and stirred vigorously with a mechanical stirrer for 15.0 min. The sorbent was held by an external magnet and the supernatant was decanted. The retained analyte on the sorbent was desorbed upon the addition of 560.0 μL of acetonitrile as the eluent and vortexing for 100 s. To determine the extracted FZD by the designed probe, 300.0 µL of S, N-GQDs was added to the extract and after 15 min of incubation, it was diluted to 1.5 mL with acetonitrile and the fluorescence intensity of the resulting solution (F) was measured. The fluorescence intensity of the blank solution prepared under the same conditions was also measured (F0) and the analytical signal was calculated according to the relation of (F0-F)/F0. The schematic of the extraction and determination process of FZD is provided in Fig. 1 .

Since FZD does not have any emission in the visible region and its concentration in real samples is very low, the development of a simple method for its preconcentration and determination is considered. In this regard, the fluorescence probe of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots was designed to measure FZD after its separation and preconcentration by magnetic solid-phase microextraction. The measurement is based on the charge transfer quenching of fluorescence intensity a phenomenon that usually occurs when the absorption spectrum of the species overlaps with the fluorophore emission spectrum. As shown in Fig. S3, the emission spectrum of S, N-GQDs (Fig. S3a) with a maximum emission of 443 nm and the absorption spectrum of FZD (Fig. S3b) with the maximum absorption at 367 nm has overlap indicating the possibility of effective interaction of these two species by the charge transfer process. This is because, the structure of S, N-GQDs is composed of aromatic hexagonal rings and as a result, its surface is rich in π electrons. When S, N-GQDs are exposed to UV radiation, π electrons are excited and holes and electrons are formed, the recombination of these electrons and holes resulted in the release of energy as radiation. The FZD antibiotic with a nitrofuran ring has a nitrogen atom as a strong electron donor that combines with the created holes in S, N-GQDs preventing the interaction of excited electrons with these holes, followed by a decrease in intensity of fluorescence of in S, N-GQDs. The quenching effect of FZD on S, N-GQDs is proportional to its concentration and is used for its measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of S, N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis spectrum of graphene quantum dots often has an absorption band in the region of 220–370 nm, due to n ⟶ π* transition of the carbonyl group (C = O) tailing to the visible region [39]. The absorption spectrum of S, N-GQDs shows an absorption band at 308 nm related to n ⟶ π* transition of the carbonyl group (C = O) and a band at 230 nm related to π ⟶ π* transition of sp2 hybrid of the aromatic ring, confirming the correct synthesis of S, N-GQD.

The size and morphology of the synthesized S, N-GQDs nanoparticles were investigated with a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The result (Fig. 2 a) illustrated that the particles have a uniform distribution with spherical shape and diameter in the range of 9–20 nm.

The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of S, N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 2b. The absorption band at 3281 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The bands at 2874 and 1369 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CH and C = C in the aromatic structure of the compound, respectively. The absorption band at 1740 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of C = O, indicating the presence of numerous oxygenated functional groups on the surface of the aromatic ring of S, N-GQDs. The absorption at 2362 and 1213 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibrations of SH and C-N, respectively, confirming the successful synthesis of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots.

The FT-IR of the oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs-ox) sorbent, magnetic multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MMWCNTs), FZD, and FZD sorbed on the oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes is reported in Fig. 3 a. The broad absorption band at 3435 cm−1 in the MWCNTs-ox spectrum is related to the stretching vibration of OH. Furthermore, the absorption peaks that appeared at 1000, 1125, and 1296 cm−1 are characteristic absorption bands of the oxygenated functional groups of C = O, OH, and C-O, respectively, indicating the success of the oxidation process of carbon nanotubes. In the spectrum of MMWCNTs, the absorption bands at 580 and 629 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrations of Fe-O and Fe-O-Fe of the magnetic nanoparticles structure of Fe3O4 indicating the right magnetization of the multi-walled carbon nanotubes. The peak at 1635 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the C = O group of carbon nanotubes. The absorption band at 3400 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of OH attached to Fe and carbon nanotubes and for this reason, has a higher intensity and wider width than that of oxidized MWCNTs. The FZD spectrum has absorption bands at 1349 and 1395 cm−1 related to the bending vibration of NO2 and the band at 1760 cm−1 corresponds to the functional group of C = O in the FZD structure. In addition, the characteristic peak of the nitrofuran structure appears at 1108 and 1249 cm−1, indicating the presence of a furan ring in the structure of FZD. The spectrum of FZD sorbed on the oxidized MMWCNTs contains the FZD absorption bands at 1760, 1395, and 1249 cm−1 indicating the successful sorption of the analyte to the sorbent. The expansion of the OH absorption band at 3410 cm−1 in this spectrum illustrates the effective interaction of the non-bonded electrons of FZD with the OH group of sorbent through hydrogen bond formation and a significant reduction in the intensity of the sorbent peak at 580 cm−1 is also an indication of analyte-sorbent interaction.

The morphology of MMWCNTs sorbent structure was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM)). The results (Fig. 3b) demonstrated the successful stabilization of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles in the form of spread bright dots among the carbon nanotubes with an average size of about 20–30 nm. Therefore, MMWCNTs can be used as nanoscale adsorbents for magnetic extraction.

The magnetic properties of magnetic multi-walled carbon nanotubes were evaluated using a vibration sample magnetometer (VSM). The S shape of the curve shows that the prepared sorbent is magnetic (Fig. 3c). The saturation magnetization (S) of MMWCNTs is 38.69 emu g−1, illustrating the high magnetic property of the sorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the effective parameters on the determination of FZD
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the optimal measurements conditions, the effect of important parameters on the interaction of FZD with S, N-GQDs such as the volume of S, N-GQDs, and the contact time between drug and quantum dots were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of S, N-GQDs volume
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of the volume of S, N-GQDs, different volumes (100.0–600.0 µL) of S, N-GQDs were diluted to the final volume of 1.5 mL with distilled water and its fluorescence intensity (F0) was measured. Then, 900.0 µL of FZD solution (3.0 mg L−1) was added to the same volume range of S, N-GQDs, the final volume was adjusted to 1.5 mL, and after 15 min its fluorescence intensity (F) was determined. The analytical signal was calculated using the relation (F0-F)/F0. With a constant concentration of FZD, the analytical signal reaches a maximum at the 300.0 µL volume of S, N-GQDs (Fig. S4 a). The decrease in the analytical signal in the volume greater than 300.0 µL can be related to the increase in the amount of S, N-GQDs relative to quencher concentration, resulting in a reduction in the analytical signal. Thus, 300.0 µL was selected as an optimum volume.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of contact time between FZD and S, N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To find the minimum time required for achieving the maximum quenching effect, the effect of contact time between FZD and S, N-GQDs on the analytical signal was considered. Thus, 300.0 µL of S, N-GQDs was added to 1200.0 µL of the FZD solution (3.0 mg L−1), and its fluorescence intensity was measured over the contact period of 2–30 min. The results (Fig. S4b) revealed that with increasing the contact time from 2 to15 minutes, the analytical signal increases, and then reaches a plateau by a further increase in contact time. Thus, the contact time of 15 min was selected as the optimum value for further experiment.

In the design of magnetic solid-phase extraction, the selection of eluent is an important task. The ideal eluent should be able to reduce the interaction of the analyte with the sorbent, quickly desorb it in a minimum amount of solvent, and also do not interface with the measurement technique. On this basis, first, a solution of 3.0 mg L−1 of furazolidone in various solvents was prepared, and its effect on the quenching of fluorescence intensity of S, N-GQDs, and the analytical signal was studied. The results of this investigation (Fig. S5a) revealed that with acetonitrile, methanol, dimethylformamide, and the mixture of acetonitrile/methanol (1:1 v/v), the analytical signal was almost the same, indicating that these solvents have no significant effect on the measurement. Then, the capability of the aforementioned solvents on desorption of FZD from the sorbent was evaluated. The result of this study (Fig. S5b) indicates that with acetonitrile, the recovery of FZD was higher than with other solvents (Table S1). FZD is a polar compound with a high affinity for hydrogen bonding and dipole–dipole interaction. As a result, highly polar solvents such as acetonitrile can more effectively recover it from the sorbent. Therefore, in the continuation of the study, acetonitrile was selected as a suitable eluent. Then, the effect of pH, eluent volume, sorption, and desorption time, as well as the amount of sorbent on extraction was optimized using the central composite design.

Experimental design methods are a set of statistical techniques that are used to simultaneously find patterns, the effects of individual variables on the analytical response, and the relationship between variables.

In the multivariate optimization of extraction method, the selection of the appropriate design for the experiment depends on the number and nature of the effective variables on the extraction. Central composite design (CCD) is one of the most widely used experimental design methods because of the advantages of fewer experiments, saving time and cost, and providing comprehensive statistical information including interactions between variables and quadratic expressions. Therefore, in this study, the central composite design was used for the optimization of the effective factor on the extraction and preconcentration of FZD. The CCD consists of two-level factorial points and axial points with at least one central point for fitting polynomial models. The purpose of providing axial points, which are determined by alpha values, is to ensure that the model is perpendicular and reproducible. To have a good estimate of the model error, the central point is usually repeated. Based on the literature survey and preliminary experiments, five factors of pH, eluent volume, the mass of sorbent, sorption time, and desorption time were selected as the more effective parameter on extraction. Then, each factor was examined at five levels using the CCD (Table 1 ). The central composite design matrix consists of a total number of 32 experiments (ten factorial points, sixteen axial points, and six central points) that were used for the construction of the second-order model. The variables were changed systematically and the order of the experiments was random to eliminate the systematic effects of the non-random order of the experiments. After the selection of variables and their levels, the experiments were performed according to conditions defined by CCD, and the results were modeled and analyzed using DE software version 11.0.5. To find the relation between response and effective variables on the response, the statistical-mathematical model of multiple linear regression (MLR) with quadratic polynomial equation was used. The general form of the MLR equation used is as follows:

Y= ƒ (x1,…, xi) =.
b 0
+
∑
i = 1
k
b i
x i
+
∑
i = 1
k
∑
j = 1
k
b
ij
x i
x j
+
∑
i = 1
k
b
ii
x
i
2

In this equation, b0 is the intercept, bi is the linear coefficient of each factor, bii is the quadratic coefficient, bij is the interaction between the parameters and Y represents the predicted relation.

The optimization method includes evaluating the effect of important parameters, estimating the coefficients, matching the data with the response functions, and controlling the performance of the model. Thus, the data of the multilevel experiments are prepared and the fitting of the curve with nonlinear responses which is usually done through regression is considered. Then, the compatible models with the responses are reported in the analysis of variance (ANOVA) table. This table includes the important parameters of the lack of fit (LOF), the square of the correlation coefficient (R2), the square of the adjusted correlation coefficient (R2 adj), the square of the predicted correlation coefficient (R2 pred), the P-value and F-value. Based on the results of ANOVA analysis, the compatibility of the model with the experimental values is determined by R2, a statistical index that shows the amount and type of relationship between variables. When a model is described with a curve instead of a line, the R2 value must be calculated to confirm the suitability of the curve.
R
2
=
S S
R E G
S S
T

In this equation, SSREG and SST are the regression sum of squares and the total sum of squares, respectively. When R2 is equal to one, the fitted line covers all the data. Although the value of R2 usually increases with increasing the degrees of the equation, the suitability of R2 for the achievement of a proper curve should not be done simply by increasing the degrees of the equation. Thus, the mentioned R2 is modified to the R2 adj, in which the average squares are used with consideration of the degree of freedom. The adjusted R2 can be negative but its value is always less than or equal to R2. The closeness of R2 adj to R2 indicates the correct selection of variables and the low relative deviation of the method. Another parameter that confirms the suitability of the proposed model is the square of the predicted correlation coefficient R2 pred, which illustrates the capability of the model for the prediction of the response. The value of R2 pred is always less than R2, but a great difference between them is an indication of the presence of insignificant factors in the model. In a suitable model, the values of R2, R2adj, and R2pred are close to one and the P-value is<0.05. The ANOVA analysis (Table 2 and Table S2) indicated that the best model for FZD extraction is the quadratic regression model with a high correlation between the coded and predicted values as the values of all three correlation coefficients R2, R2 Adj, and R2 Pred are close to one (Table S2),. Thus, the selected model has a good capability for correlating between independent factors and response.

The results obtained by analysis of variance using CCD with the quadratic regression model are provided in Table 2 and Table S2. The quadratic statistical parameters indicate that the regression is statistically significant and the p-value related to lack of model fit (0.9863) can be ignored. From the statistical point of view, the net error of the model is insignificant and the curvature is sufficient.

The general equation for the effective parameters on the FZD extraction and the interaction between them obtained after analysis of variance are follows; where Xs are the same factors defined in Table 1.

One of the ways to evaluate the efficiency of the model is to consider the residual values curve, a quantity that expresses the difference between the predicted and observed values. The normal probability curve demonstrates the normality of the data and confirms the appropriateness of the model (Fig. S6).

The plot of the residual values versus the number of tests (Fig. S7) indicates that data distribution does not follow a specific pattern, and there is no systematic error in the data. The data are uniformly distributed around the X-axis, confirming the appropriateness of the proposed model. Concerning the selected levels, the range of distribution of data is also desirable.
The 3D response plots were utilized to evaluate the interaction among parameters, achieve the maximum response, and obtain the optimum value of each parameter.

In MSPE, the pH of the solution is one of the most important factors that can affect the sorbent and analytes properties and thereby controlling the analyte recovery. Considering the chemical structure of FZD, the pH value has a great effect on its predominant form. In the basic solution, the FZD is not stable and decomposes, so based on the pKa value of FZD (5.5), the effect of pH on its extraction in the range of 4.5–7.5, where its molecular form is dominant, was investigated.

Magnetic solid-phase extraction is an equilibrium process and the transfer of species between two phases requires sufficient contact time between two phases for extraction of the maximum amount of species. The effect of contact time in the range of 5–30 min was investigated. The changes in the extraction of FZD as the function of pH and sorption time provided in Fig. 4 a indicate that the extraction recovery increases with increasing pH up to 6, and then decreases. The increase of recovery in acidic pHs can be attributed to the protonation of the sorbent, increasing its interaction with non-bonded electrons of FZD. It also shows that the maximum extraction of FZD requires a contact time of 15 min.

The amount of sorbent also has a significant effect on the quantitative extraction of the analyte. The response curve of the interaction between pH and the amount of sorbent and its effect on FZD recovery (Fig. 4b) revealed that the recovery increases with decreasing pH to 5.25 and increasing the amount of sorbent to 30.0 mg. Thus, 30.0 mg of sorbent was chosen as the optimum amount.

The response curve of the interaction between sorption time and eluent volume and their effect on the recovery of FZD (Fig. 4c) indicates that with increasing the sorption time up to 15 min and the eluent volume up to 560.0 µL, the recovery reaches its maximum value.

The effect of the interaction of time of desorption and volume of eluent on the response surface curve (Fig. 4d) demonstrates that the recovery reaches its maximum with 560.0 µL eluent volume and 96 s desorption time.

In designing a separation and preconcentration system, the achievement of a high preconcentration factor is desirable for obtaining a low detection limit. The high preconcentration factor can be obtained by increasing the sample volume and decreasing the eluent volume. Therefore, the extraction of a fixed amount of FZD (6.0 µg) from the volume of 50.0–250.0 mL was investigated. The results (Fig. S8a) show that by increasing the sample volume up to 150.0 mL the recovery of FZD is constant and then decreases at higher sample volumes. So, 150.0 mL was chosen as the optimum volume.

Ionic strength is another factor that may affect extraction efficiency. The effect of ionic strength on the extraction efficiency of FZD was evaluated by varying the concentration of NaCl (0–2.0 mol L−1). The result of this consideration shows that the presence of salt up to 0.5 mol L−1 does not have any significant influence on the extraction of FZD but a further increase in the concentration of NaCl causes a decrease in the extraction of FZD (Fig. S8b). The decrease in the recovery at higher salt concentrations can be related to the mass transfer limitation of FZD to the sorbent due to the reduction of FZD activity as well as the interaction of chlorine ions of the aqueous phase with the hydrogens of the sorbent surface.

The selectivity of the measurement method was evaluated. For this purpose, 1200.0 µL of drugs (3.0 mg L−1) with a structure similar to FZD such as nitrofurazone, nitrofurantoin, carbamazepine, and diclofenac were added to 300.0 µL of S, N-GQDs and after 5 min of incubation, the analytical signals was measured. The results (Fig. S9a) revealed that the quenching of S, N-GQDs fluorescence intensity in the presence of FZD is much higher than other drugs. Thus, the constructed nanoprobe using S, N-GQDs possesses a high selectivity in the determination of FZD. Furthermore, the selectivity of the designed extraction and measurement system was investigated by extraction and measurement of 40.0 µg L−1 of the aforementioned drugs from 150.0 mL of the sample by the proposed method. The results (Fig. S9b) show the high selectivity of the method for FZD quantification.

The figures of merit of the developed method for the extraction and measurement of FZD were investigated. The linear range of the method was determined by extracting different concentrations of FZD from 150.0 mL of the solution under optimal conditions, and measurement of the extracted FZD by the constructed nanoprobe. Good linearity between the analytical signal and the concentration of FZD within the range of 8.0–250.0 µg L−1 with the calibration equation of (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0023C + 0.0481 and coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.9957 was obtained (Fig. 5 ). In this equation, F0 and F represent the emission of quantum dots in the absence and presence of furazolidone, respectively, and C is the concentration of furazolidone (µg L−1). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ), determined as 3sb/m and 10sb/m (where sb is the standard deviation of the blank solution and m is the slope of the calibration graph), were 2.13 and 7.10 µg L−1, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for intra and inter-day of six independent FZD (at 40 µg L−1 level) determinations were 3.6 and 5.6% respectively, indicating the good precision of the method for FZD measurement. The enhancement factor determined from the ratio of the slops of calibration curves with and without preconcentration was found to be 97. The results of this study are summarized in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method for real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the method for quantification of FZD in real samples was studied by extracting and measuring FZD in complex matrices such as chicken liver, poultry effluent, urine, and plasma samples (Fig. S10). The accuracy of the method was evaluated by considering the recoveries of the FZD added at three concentration levels to the samples as well as comparing the results with the results of independent analysis by HPLC. The results of this investigation (Table 3 ) show that the recoveries of the analyte at three concentration levels from different samples are in the range of 93.8–104.4%, indicating that the matrix of the samples does not have a significant effect on the determination of FZD. Furthermore, at a 95% confidence level, there is no significant difference between the results of the two methods. Thus, the developed method is suitable for monitoring and determining FZD in these types of samples. In addition, the method was applied to the extraction and measurement of the FZD in the tablet with a claimed amount of 100.0 mg drug per tablet. The amount of FZD in each tablet was found as 97.60 ± 2.31. Thus, at 95% confidence level, there is no significant difference between the amount determined by the developed method, and the claimed value (the experimental t value is 2.39 which is less than the tabulated t value of 2.78, n = 5) showing the accuracy of the method is acceptable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the developed method with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: The figures of the merit of the method were compared with some recently reported methods for the determination of FZD. The results of this comparison (Table 4 ) revealed that the developed method enjoyed the use of the simpler and more accessible instrument, and has a linear range and detection limit that are comparable to other reported methods for the determination of FZD. Moreover, the method has the capability of the extraction and measurement of trace amounts of FZD in complex tissues.

Herein, graphene quantum dots doped with sulfur and nitrogen were synthesized and an optical sensor for the determination of FZD based on the fluorescence quenching of synthesized quantum dots was designed. FZD was separated and preconcentrated from chicken liver, poultry effluent, urine, and blood plasma samples by magnetic solid-phase microextraction method using MMWCNTs sorbent and measured by the designed fluorescence probe. The optical and structural properties of synthesized S, N-GQDs were investigated by absorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis), fluorescence, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The structural properties of the sorbent were also confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), and vibration sample magnetometer (VSM). The proposed extraction and measurement method of furazolidone possesses the advantages of simplicity, good selectivity, wide linear range, low detection limit, and the ability to determine trace amounts of furazolidone in complex matrices.

Sepideh Saadatmandi Manshadi: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shayessteh Dadfarnia: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ali Mohammad Haji Shabani: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Roya Afsharipour: Writing – review & editing. Sadegh Jabbaran: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107439.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of an electrochemical sensor based on a functionalized carbon black/tungsten carbide hybrid composite for the detection of furazolidone
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.Y. Hwa, T.S. Sharma, P. Karuppaiah, Development of an electrochemical sensor based on a functionalized carbon black/tungsten carbide hybrid composite for the detection of furazolidone, New J. Chem. 43 (2019) 12078-12086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hollow fiber reinforced with molecularly imprinted polymer supported on multiwalled carbon nanotubes for microextraction of furazolidone in real samples prior to its spectrophotometric determination
J. Iran. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Toudeshki, A.M. Shabani, S. Dadfarnia, Hollow fiber reinforced with molecularly imprinted polymer supported on multiwalled carbon nanotubes for microextraction of furazolidone in real samples prior to its spectrophotometric determination, J. Iran. Chem. Soc. 16 (2019) 2003-2010.
